paragraphs.body: Unraveling the influence of instagram reels on society Addicting social media adhd enabling websites such as tiktok, youtube shorts, and snapchat spotlight, have been rapidly growing in popularity since the initial debut of short video scrolling in 2016. Alongside the rise of short scrolling videos also arose worry and fear in relation to the negative effects these websites have on the human mind young and old apart. However, all fear and worry about these apps becomes negligible after an individual’s first experience using instagram reels. Instagram reels, whilst also being a short video scrolling app, doubles as a sinful nursery for nonchalant disrespect and humorous self clowning. Although it’s fair to say that not all videos on instagram reels are horrible or sinful, enough are that it makes a negative impact on society for those who use it, possibly one that should raise an eyebrow or two.

annotations.text: as a sinful nursery for nonchalant disrespect and humorous self clowning.

paragraphs.body: The toxicity generated from instagram reels mainly comes from a combination between creators knowingly or unknowingly making a fool of themselves and the commenters on each video sharing their questionable opinions. A good example would be ‘untitled video’ by damianterantera. In ‘untitled video’ we see an example of a guy dancing with noticeable details of him having a facial structure similar to those with ‘down syndrome’ and him having a haircut called the ‘edgar cut’. This is relevant, because once we open the comment section the very first comment we see is: “spedgar” - from user; ‘username1’, which is a derogatory combination of the words ‘sped’ and edgar’. If you thought this was offensive, wait till you read the other comments. “Music9/10, Dance10/10, Chromosome 78/76,” - from user; ‘username2’. This comment is generally rating the videos’ music and dance, while cunningly adding in a rating of 78/76 chromosomes, making fun of the idea that this guy may have more chromosomes than a ‘medically normal’ person. These two comments generally cover the extent of rude language in the comment section stemming from rude comments. However, it is important to note that there are hundreds of comments just like these two on this video alone that would take a milenia to cover. In their fundamental reddit comment describing instagram reels, Instagram reel comments are just embarrassing, user; username7 complains about the lack of humanity in the reels they watch. According to Starry, “You could open up any reel feed and see these prepubescent anti-shower teenage boys or misogynistic attention seeking teenage girls bullying someone for quite literally existing.”(1). In making this comment, Starry argues the ridiculous and gross behavior of commenters on instagram reels. This all said of course, in vain, attempting to bring light to the toxic unpunished behavior of those nasty commenters using instagram reels.

annotations.text: According to Starry, “You could open up any reel feed and see these prepubescent anti-shower teenage boys or misogynistic attention seeking teenage girls bullying someone for quite literally existing.”(1). In making this comment, Starry argues the ridiculous and gross behavior of commenters on instagram reels.

paragraphs.body: Of course it’s always important to understand the difference between humor and negativity. Did I laugh a little whilst reading the comments of the dancing video, yes. Does it being somewhat funny mean it’s okay to write those comments? Absolutely not. In an untitled video by user - ‘username3’ we have a video that seems to teeter on the line between being funny and offensive. The video in question seems to contain a possibly inebriated young girl doing god knows what on camera. The comment section of this video contains multiple pictures or gifs intended to make fun of the girl in a joking manner (possible jumpscare warning, timid audiences stay advised), (image links can also be found in citations): image1, image2, image3, image4, image5. Whether these images are offensive and should be removed or are joking and funny making them okay is up for debate, especially considering the girl in question seemed to be making fun of herself in the first place. Whether it’s a healthy practice or not to clown yourself for attention is also another important question. One comment on the video from user: ‘username4’ goes as follows: “1 day no fried chicken I’m seeing changes already”. This comment refers to the idea that she resembles a stereotypical black person of lighter skin color, and is thus reliant on fried chicken in order to maintain her sanity. Another comment from user: ‘username5’ has this to say: “Albinigga”. This comment I believe is a combination of the ‘n-word’ and the word ‘albino’ used to mock the girl for her appearance. Regardless of whether or not these comments are funny to me as an individual, it is obvious that they are funny to many other people. It’s important to discern the difference between funny and offensive.

annotations.text: Whether it’s a healthy practice or not to clown yourself for attention is also another important question.
annotations.comment: This is an interesting comment. Let's discuss!
this is a truly offensive term. I might edit it for the essay...

paragraphs.body: The question to ask in relevance to this issue is whether or not its okay to post comments like this on videos making fun of people, and whether or not it’s okay to post videos clowning oneself in the first place. In a reddit post, I find it distasteful to pay people in poor communities to be clowns for our entertainment, user - username6 sheds light on a website which allows for the buyer to pay for a video made from people in poorer communities to make fun of themselves. As the prominent philosopher Ace puts it, “My immediate reaction is that this is distasteful and perhaps even dystopian but when you dig into it a bit more, you see that this is a charity that is trying to help these people who live in poor communities. Is it taking advantage of someone to pay them to beclown themselves for our entertainment? It feels like it is.” In other words, Ace believes that it’s important to figure out if it’s okay or morally correct to practice paying people to clown themselves or rewarding people for clowning themselves. This individual further argues that there may be coercion involved making the practice immoral, because the person may feel coerced into clowning themselves considering they have no other way of making a living. He finalizes the post by claiming that he doesn’t like the idea of it but he may want to reconsider the positive side of it, although he does seem confused as to what exactly the positive side is.

annotations.text: In other words, Ace believes that it’s important to figure out if it’s okay or morally correct to practice paying people to clown themselves or rewarding people for clowning themselves.

annotations.comment: I just watched an episode on the old TV show _Medium_ about this very thing.
annotations.context_left: er the positive side of it, although he does seem confused as to what exactly the positive side is.

paragraphs.body: All in all, a serious discussion needs to actualize on the morality of instagram reels in and of itself, and the practice of self clowning. It’s especially important to discuss the nonchalant toxicity that is shown on said videos on instagram reels. Rude and nonchalant comments can make someone die of laughter or it can ruin someone's self esteem, drawing a fine line between the two is vital for a prominent future of humanity as a whole. In the first example video of the guy dancing, if he were to read those comments he may end up really depressed or upset with himself for the way the comments describe him. On the other hand, many people might have gotten a good laugh out of said rude comments making their days better. In the second video of the girl doing god knows what, she would have most likely been unbothered by the rude comments or even laughing over them considering she was knowingly making a fool of herself in the first place. Likewise, the viewers or commenters might have also found the comments funny making themselves happier as well. Developing a brighter understanding of these practices as a whole is a vital effort in order to save the self esteem of those unknowingly or knowingly being clowned on, or those who are clowning themselves.

annotations.text: All in all, a serious discussion needs to actualize
